slides.title: Telemetry on HTTPS RR and QUIC v2 adoption

chat.author: Behcet Sarikaya
chat.text: Hi

chat.author: Behcet Sarikaya
chat.text: No 126 on Zulip yet

chat.author: Behcet Sarikaya
chat.text: OK now I see it

chat.author: Alan Frindell
chat.text: technically, the meta implementation is in proxygen (HTTP/WT library), not mvfst (quic)

chat.author: Marco Munizaga
chat.text: The QMux impl is in proxygen? No changes in mvfst?

chat.author: Lucas Pardue
chat.text: oops sorry

chat.author: Alan Frindell
chat.text: +1 any suitable

chat.author: Alan Frindell
chat.text:
The QMux impl is in proxygen? No changes in mvfst?

That is correct.  We bascially decided it was easier to make this part of our WT over H2 impl than re-use the mvfst parser/framer.  YMMV

chat.author: Matt Joras
chat.text: mvfst will not dignify the existence of stream based substrates

chat.author: Alan Frindell
chat.text: See what I have to deal with ??

chat.author: Nicholas Jones
chat.text: One feature of HTTP over TLS: SNI - which keys the certificate and then sets a context boundary for the connection.  But maybe this is not important to a qmux user, so perhaps a 'crypto-less' variant with different ALPN

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: Distinct ports seems like a non-starter for most of the Internet, right?

chat.author: Mirja Kühlewind
chat.text: don't use ports...

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: @Mirja bullet two, slide 11?

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: under options

chat.author: Yaroslav Rosomakho
chat.text:
I wonder if we should have an HTTP upgrade token for QMux.
QMux-over-TLS-TCP will not go through opinionated  middleboxes

chat.author: Etienne Dechamps
chat.text: Worth noting that "classic" TLS over TCP is likely to traverse middleboxes just fine, whereas the same can't be said of QUIC CRYPTO frames.

chat.author: Mirja Kühlewind
chat.text: yes, sorry the "u" got lost when typing. I meant: don't use ports

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: I feel like rather than saying "it's a you problem" I'd say "it's a substrate problem", and if someone wants to write up qmux over unix domain sockets that can be a separate spec

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: (Not clear to me if this means file descriptors or named sockets; the latter seems maybe easier since you have the names?)

chat.author: Kazuho Oku
chat.text: as to HTTP upgrade, I think upgrade token specifies the application protocol, whereas QMux is a substrate for some of those

chat.author: Alan Frindell
chat.text: Yeah I think I'm convinced by that argument Jonathan.  Maybe it's a bit of text that gets repeated if say, we have Unix domain and some other non-tls substrate but it's also not that much duplication

chat.author: altanai
chat.text: maybe this is dumb question but a malicious peer could request EKU repeatedly to consume CPU or memory right ?

chat.author: Marco Munizaga
chat.text: re HTTP upgrade, would these opinionated middleboxes not also block unknown upgrade protocols?

chat.author: Christian Huitema
chat.text: If we think long duration sessions, shall we do something about 2^62?

chat.author: Magnus Westerlund
chat.text: @Altanai yes, but I think this is a implementation warning that you should monitor for unneccessary usage and in that case termiante the connection.

chat.author: Lucas Pardue
chat.text: many QUIC features can be similarly abused - the EKU draft can borrow text from RFC9000 security considerations if if doesn't already have it

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: Now I'm worried if the quic for tiptop draft has to worry about your peer's clock running significantly faster or slower due to general relativity.

chat.author: Lucas Pardue
chat.text: depends if you mvfst Jonathan

chat.author: Magnus Westerlund
chat.text: Christian I don't think it is that necessary. 2^62 is a large number. Just sending one 1 gbps over one stream it will take almost 1170 years before you exhaust the stream frame offset value. The packet number is simular hard to exhaust

chat.author: John Preuß Mattsson
chat.text: ML-KEM-768 is very fast. You can do around 75,000 encapsulations on a single core. the X25519 part slows in down, but still fast enough. If the peer is asking for 10 EKU per second you should probably just terminate

chat.author: Marten Seemann
chat.text: I'm not putting the stream ID in the unencrypted part of the header. Not going to happen

chat.author: Christian Huitema
chat.text: So this is a perf tradeoff, more complexity for less memcpy. But I don't see memcpy as a huge performance issue...

chat.author: Kazuho Oku
chat.text: yeah as said on Mic, I think I like the simplicity on the sending side, that you can build data then write the frame length using varint format

chat.author: Kyle Nekritz
chat.text: With a stream cipher you could probably achieve this with incremental decryption too?

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: Marten: does the spec say that?  Or are you inferring that you need to know the stream ID before you know where you want to put the decrypted data?

chat.author: Marco Munizaga
chat.text: +1 ian's comment :)

chat.author: Martin Duke
chat.text: In the abstract I support doing cool new things with versioning

chat.author: Brian Trammell
chat.text: ian said what i wanted to better and louder :)

chat.author: Matt Joras
chat.text: +1 if you are going to go after perf get the chainsaw out and see how far you get

chat.author: Ask Hansen
chat.text: Without comment on the specific idea in the reverso draft, the number of memory copies required in QUIC is limiting some high bandwidth (hundreds of gbps per server) use cases for us.

chat.author: Matt Joras
chat.text: Ask: that is almost certainly a function of packet size, no?

chat.author: Ask Hansen
chat.text: Matt: I'll leave the details to @Craig Taylor, but some workloads the resource use is dominated by copying bits around inside the computer and each copy is some percentage less bits that can make it from cache to the network card. Netflix have made some good presentations on their work minimizing this for regular TCP TLS on FreeBSD.

chat.author: Matt Joras
chat.text: Yeah I would interrogate if there are actually more copies or if this is an implementation/integration problem for you

chat.author: Marco Munizaga
chat.text: Love the strength of your conviction lucas xD

chat.author: Craig Taylor
chat.text: Betteridge's Law?

chat.author: Mirja Kühlewind
chat.text: it's not peach kucha yet, right?

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: Now I'm worried about general relativity again

chat.author: Christian Huitema
chat.text: It is not about traversing NAT, it is just recognizing that if the app was idle for 30sec, the NAT amy well have removed the bindings already.

chat.author: Christian Huitema
chat.text: Just what we need, add more complexity because it might be useful in some unknown use case...

chat.author: Mike Bishop
chat.text: Part of why we want this is to avoid sending the CONNECTION_CLOSE and waking up the client radio to tell it that you're going away.

chat.author: Valentin Goșu
chat.text: Firefox is seeing 0% as idle timeout close reason

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: @Chairs ruling out technical discussion in a WG meeting seems like an odd choice.

chat.author: altanai
chat.text: TIL this kind of data exists. thanks for sharing this  @Valentin

chat.author: Valentin Goșu
chat.text:
Lucas Pardue said:

0?!

Assuming we're recording that correctly.

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: Ted: I feel like it is the moral equivalent to a mini-BOF deciding whether a problem statement is a problem worth solving

chat.author: Lucas Pardue
chat.text: @ted the intentaon was not to dive too much into the technical merits of any particular approach (both have been presented to the WG before) but to instead understand if the problem might have shifted in light of things such as MoQ. If you have technical questions and we have time, please do ask at the mic

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: @Jonathan The security of a multicast TLS handshake in the QUIC context is something that makes a lot of difference between "intriguing " and "worrying".  Ruling it out in favor of a bland assertion that it's working and secure doesn't match my expectations.

chat.author: Matt Joras
chat.text: 0% idle timeout defines belief tbh

chat.author: Brian Trammell
chat.text: yeah that feels like a data collection issue

chat.author: Valentin Goșu
chat.text: CC @Max Inden

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: Ted: I think the model is to do TLS handshake unicast and then send a frame indicating which multicast group to connect to (and the encryption key).  This is something I'm more confident about, though it would still need analysis of course

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: In MoQ, where you are talking to a relay, that's a lot more likely to work and this slide is the first that looks  above the  "intriguing" bar.

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: (Sorry slide 9)

chat.author: Magnus Westerlund
chat.text: I think the only security issue, is that any client in the session can potentially extract the key and use it to inject packets into other receivers. But multicast authenticity requires either non-assymetric cryptography or some possibly TESLA authentication mechanism which is time based.

chat.author: Magnus Westerlund
chat.text: Sorry, should not have written only, meant the main

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: It looks like it is single-source multicast from relay to the clients.

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: I think the key here is that this isn't pure multicast, this is unicast control with multicast data transmission add-on.  This means you can send something on the unicast channel to verify the validity of the multicast chanel

chat.author: Magnus Westerlund
chat.text: Yes, that is possible solution thanks to the hybrid.

chat.author: Alan Frindell
chat.text: This is very interesting

chat.author: Craig Taylor
chat.text: ...and for a retransmission server

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: @Magnus, Jonathan agreed

chat.author: Mirja Kühlewind
chat.text: looking at other transport parameters (now that the RFC loaded) there are actually many that only provide an initial value. some are only useful at start. so not many left but there could be a few more than just max idle timeout.

chat.author: Gorry Fairhurst
chat.text: My question wasn't intended to suggest that CC was a blockage - there is a feedback path, and some challenges ahead, but looks like it could be useful!

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: I think this might benefit from an actual BoF; this was a teaser trailer, and real data would be needed to know whether the work should be taken up at this time.

chat.author: Max Inden
chat.text: Regarding 0% idle timeout shared above by Valentin, this is a bug in our instrumentation. Given that idle close is local, we never enter closing state, but jump straight to closed state. Problem is, in the former do we register the close reason metric. I can prepare a patch, but won't have data today or tomorrow.

chat.author: Gorry Fairhurst
chat.text: @Ted - interesting... I wonder if  we will find "protocols" "sec" and operators/providers wanting the technology...

chat.author: François Michel
chat.text: You can do unicast retransmission, OR you can do FEC. :-)

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: Or you can do multicast retransmission if it turns out a packet got dropped very close to the multicast sender

chat.author: François Michel
chat.text: Definitely!

chat.author: Marco Munizaga
chat.text:
A comment in chat since we're at time on the NAT Traversal presentation:

Marten and I just updated our Alternative Server Address Draft: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-munizaga-quic-alternative-server-address/01/
I think the alternative server address extension serves as a building block for a separate NAT traversal doc.
